segments.s479-g49: همو is an emphatic demonstrative/pronoun 'that same one' or 'he himself' and stands as the sentence topic. آدمی is the noun 'man/human'. بودکان is an archaic fused form (literally bud + kān) introducing a relative or identifying clause ('who was' / 'who is'), so the whole clause reads roughly 'he was that man who...'. چهره داشت is a simple past construction 'had a face/appearance' where داشت is the past-tense/perfect auxiliary meaning 'had' and چهره is the direct object.
segments.s479-g50: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'in (his) goodness/beauty' (به + noun used adverbially). ز هر اختری is a prepositional partitive 'from every star' (ز governs the following noun with هر 'every'). بهره داشت is the verb phrase 'had a share' (بهره = 'share/portion', داشت = past 'had'); the verb ties the whole phrase as the predicate describing what he possessed.
segments.s479-g51: بدو گفت رومی is inverted classical word order meaning 'the Roman said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت here functions as 'said'). که‌ای شهریار begins the direct speech; که (here) introduces what is being said and ای شهریار is the vocative 'O king.'
segments.s479-g52: در ایوان 'in the iwan/portico' (در = 'in', ایوان = 'iwan/porch'). چنو is an archaic/poetic variant of چنان 'such/so', modifying نگار ('such a beloved/beauty'). کس نبیند is a negated generic clause: کس here functions as 'anyone/no one' (negative sense), and نبیند (present negative 3rd person) expresses 'does not see' / 'no one sees'. The whole segment conveys 'in the iwan no one sees such a beauty.'
segments.s479-g53: کنون is an adverb 'now' setting the temporal frame. هر یکی از is a partitive 'each one of...' (هر = 'each', یکی = 'one', از = 'of/from'). یک اندام ماه is a descriptive noun phrase 'a moon‑formed body/figure' (اندام = 'figure/body', ماه = 'moon' used metaphorically to mean moon‑like beauty). Together they mean 'now, each one of the moon‑formed (figures)...'.
segments.s479-g54: فرستیم is 1st‑person plural past/optative form of فرستادن; in context with کنون it reads as a hortative/let‑us construction 'let (each) send' or an instruction 'we/let them send'. یک نامه is the direct object 'a single letter'. نزدیک شاه uses نزدیک ('near') adverbially/locatively with شاه ('king') to mean 'to the king' or 'into the king's presence.'
segments.s479-g55: نشستند پس فیلسوفان بهم: نشستند is the past plural 'they sat (down)' or 'they seated themselves'; پس = 'then/afterwards'; فیلسوفان = 'the philosophers'; بهم = 'together/with one another'. The phrase means 'then the philosophers sat together.'
segments.s479-g56: گرفتند is the past plural 'they took/grasped'. The list قرطاس و قیر و قلم are direct objects in series: قرطاس = 'paper/sheet', و = 'and' (connector), قیر = 'tar/pitch' (here used as the dark substance for writing), و = 'and', قلم = 'pen'. The whole clause describes them taking writing materials.
segments.s479-g57: The sentence begins with the past plural verb نوشتند “they wrote,” followed by the distributive subject هر موبدی “each mobad/priest.” ز آنک + دید is a compressed relative/causal phrase meaning “of/what he saw”; ز here has the poetic sense “from/concerning,” آنک is the archaic relative ‘which/that,’ and دید is the simple past ‘he saw’ referring back to هر موبدی (each priest wrote what he himself had seen).
segments.s479-g58: که introduces the content clause ‘that …’. قرطاس is the object ‘sheet/paper.’ ز here again means ‘from/through’ (poetic), انقاس is used in the sense of ruin/damage, شد is the past of شدن “became,” and ناپدید is the predicate adjective ‘vanished/gone’ — together: ‘that the paper became ruined and disappeared.’
segments.s479-g59: ز نزدیک ایشان is a prepositional phrase ‘from near them’ (ز = from, نزدیک = near, ایشان = them). سواری is the subject ‘a horseman/rider.’ برفت is an older past form meaning ‘rode off / went away’ (verb + prefix بر- used in classical narrative to indicate motion).
segments.s479-g60: به نزد سکندر is a standard phrase ‘to the presence of Alexander’ (به = to, نزد = in the presence of, سکندر = Alexander). The following به میلاد تفت repeats the directional به and names a place or person (میلاد تفت); the double به simply strings two destinations/recipients together. میلاد and تفت are proper-name-ish here rather than literal ‘birth’ or the verb ‘to scorch,’ so the exact lexical senses are lexicalized as names in context.
segments.s479-g61: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when.’ شاه جهان is a compound title ‘the king of the world.’ نامه‌هاشان is the object ‘their letters’ (نامه + plural -ها + possessive شان). بخواند is the classical third-person past form of خواندن; in narrative it reads as simple past ‘he read.’
segments.s479-g62: ز here means ‘because of/at’ and introduces the cause of astonishment: گفتارشان ‘their speech/words.’ در شگفتی بماند is a classical-style predicate ‘he remained in wonder’ (در = in, شگفتی = astonishment, بماند = remained/was left — a present-form used in narrative sense ‘remained’).
segments.s479-g63: به نامه ‘in the letter’ (به = in/with the document, here marking the medium). هر اندام را is a topicalized/definite object ‘each limb/feature’ with را marking the object. زو is an archaic contraction of ز + او meaning ‘of him/about him’ (here indicating whose limbs are being described). یکی means ‘one’ or ‘one by one’ — the phrase signals that they give a single descriptive trait for each member.
segments.s479-g64: صفت is the noun ‘description/attribute.’ کرده بودند is the past perfect/pluperfect ‘they had made/described’ (کرده + بودند). لیک is an archaic conjunction ‘but,’ and اندکی is an adverb ‘a little/briefly’ — together: ‘they had described (each feature), but only a little.’
segments.s479-g25: This clause gives the temporal setting: 'دگر روز' = 'the next/another day' followed by a temporal subordinate 'چون آسمان گشت زرد' = 'when the sky became yellow.' Word order is typical poetry: temporal adverb + subordinate introduced by چون. گشت is the past of گشتن meaning 'became/turned.'
segments.s479-g26: برآهیخت is a past active verb 'rose up/was roused.' The following two nouns form a compound idea: خورشید = 'the sun' and تیغ نبرد = 'sword of battle' (نبرد used as a noun modifying تیغ). The verb takes the subject 'خورشید' and the phrase 'تیغ نبرد' as the thing raised.
segments.s479-g27: بیاراست here is a past transitive verb 'adorned/arrayed.' The phrase follows a somewhat emphatic word order with the verb before the object 'آن دختر شاه را' — 'that daughter of the king' marked by the object marker را.
segments.s479-g28: نباید is an impersonal modal 'it should not / need not' (here negative necessity). خود + آراستن expresses 'to adorn oneself' (reflexive/own), and ماه را ('the moon' with object marker) is the semantic topic: 'the moon did not need to adorn herself.' The clause thus states a judgment about the moon's lack of need to be adorned.
segments.s479-g29: This clause uses به + location phrase to mark direction/place: 'به خانه درون' = 'into/within the inner chamber.' تخت زرین is the direct object 'a golden throne,' and نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed/put.' The verb comes at the end of the clause as is common in Persian.
segments.s479-g30: به گرد اندر is a poetic locative cluster meaning 'around/about,' followed by آرایش چین 'folds/pleats of ornamentation.' Again نهاد is the past 'placed.' چین here means 'folds' (cloth folds), so the clause says he placed decorative folds around (the throne/person).
segments.s479-g31: نشست is the past of نشستن 'sat (down).' The prepositional cluster 'از بر تخت' is an archaic/poetic way to indicate position 'on the throne' (بر تخت), with از here governed by poetic syntax. خورشید چهر is a nominal phrase 'sun-faced' = 'with a face like the sun,' attributing brilliance to her face.
segments.s479-g32: ز is the poetic genitive/partitive 'than/from,' introducing the comparison source ناهید (Venus/Anahita). تابنده‌تر is the comparative 'more shining/more radiant' and بر سپهر 'upon the firmament/sky' locates Venus — the clause says her face was more radiant than Venus in the sky.
segments.s479-g33: برفتند is past-plural verb 'they went/they departed' (prefix-less classical past with plural ending -ند). The following words qualify the people who left: بیدار ('awake/alert') describes their state, and نه + مرد پیر negates that they were old men ('not old men'). Word order is somewhat inverted: verb first, then predicates/subjects and negation.
segments.s479-g34: زبان چرب is a noun + adjective phrase meaning literally 'a greasy/flattering tongue' (i.e., a glib or flattering speech). The coordinating و links the following role-nouns گوینده ('speaker, orator') and یادگیر ('one who learns, memorizer'), so the line lists qualities: fluent/pleasant speech, speakerly skill, and ability to learn/remember.
segments.s479-g35: فرستادشان is past 'sent them' with the object clitic -شان attached; the subject شاه follows it (inverted order common in classical Persian: verb before subject). سوی introduces the destination 'عروس' ('toward the bride').
segments.s479-g36: بر here functions as 'upon/at' indicating the cause or occasion ('at the voice/summons of ...'). آواز is 'voice/call' meaning 'summons' in this context; اسکندر فیلقوس is a compound personal name (Alexander Filqous).
segments.s479-g37: بدیدند is past plural 'they saw'. پیران ('the elders') is the subject placed after the verb (verb–subject order). رخ دخت شاه is the object: رخ ('face') followed by دخت شاه ('the king's daughter'), the possessive relationship indicated by the sequence 'رخِ دختِ شاه'.
segments.s479-g38: درفشان ('shining, glittering') characterizes the face (or the scene); ازو ('from/of her') links that brilliance to the princess. The nouns یاره, تخت, و گاه are coordinated elements describing what attended her: a companion/attendant, a throne, and a seat/place (گاه used poetically for a place/occasion).
segments.s479-g39: فرو ماندند combines the prefix فرو- ('down/deeply') with ماندند ('they remained') to mean 'they were overcome/they remained down' — a poetic way to say they were struck or stood motionless. اندرو ('in it/there') gives the location, خیره ('staring/astonished') describes their state, and خیر here is a short exclamatory/poetic word (often 'good' or an intensifier) used in older verse to mark the sentiment.
segments.s479-g40: ز is the classical preposition 'from/because of'; دیدار ('the sight/meeting') paired with او ('her') gives 'at/from the sight of her'. سست شد is a two-word predicate: سست ('weak') + شد ('became') = 'became weak'. پای پیر ('the foot of the elder') names whose foot failed — Persian often uses the possessed noun (پای) before the possessor (پیر) in such expressions.
segments.s479-g65: بدیشان is the dative prepositional phrase be‑ + dishān, ‘to them’ (placed before the subject for emphasis). جهاندار is the subject ‘the sovereign/king’. پاسخ نوشت is a light noun + past tense verb 'wrote (a) reply' (پاسخ as the object/description of what was written + نوشت, 3rd person past). Word order is topical object → subject → verb, common in epic Persian.
segments.s479-g66: The initial که is a complementizer linking the reported speech to 'نوشت' ('that/—'), then بخ‌بخ is an exclamatory interjection meaning something like 'how bounteous/blest' or an exclamation of good fortune. The second که again introduces the reported clause 'that ...'; دیدم is 1st‑person past 'I saw' (‑م = 'I'), and خرم بهشت is an adjective + noun 'a joyful/blissful paradise' (adjective precedes the noun).
segments.s479-g67: کنون 'now' sets the time. بازگردید is 2nd‑person plural (or polite) imperative/command 'return'. با چار چیز is an instrumental/associative prepositional phrase 'with four things' — چار is the cardinal 'four' modifying چیز (thing).
segments.s479-g68: برین is the classical/poetic combination of بر + ین meaning 'on/over these' or 'beyond these'; here it points back to the items just named. بر فزونی uses بر 'over/beyond' + فزونی 'increase/excess' meaning 'beyond increase' or simply 'beyond (them)'. مجویید is the negative jussive/imperative 'do not seek' (2nd‑person plural form), and نیز is a particle 'also/indeed' functioning as emphasis. Together: 'do not seek increase beyond these.'
segments.s479-g69: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' (introducing a temporal clause). منشور و عهد من is a coordinated noun phrase 'my charter and my covenant' (منشور and عهد both governed by the possessive من). او را is the definite object 'him' (را marks the direct object), and دهید is 2nd‑person plural/polite present or subjunctive/imperative 'you give' (or 'you shall give') — the verb of the temporal clause.
segments.s479-g70: شما is the explicit subject 'you' (carrying over from the previous clause). با is the instrumental preposition 'with/by'. فغستان is a noun (here a place/name or descriptive noun; treated as the instrument/item to be used). بنه is a noun (here the thing to be placed/used with فغستان), and برنهید is 2nd‑person plural imperative/subjunctive from برنهیدن 'place upon / clothe / equip' — the verb takes او را from the previous clause as its object (elliptically carried). The sequence gives an instruction: 'you, with X, place a benah upon him / clothe him.'
segments.s479-g71: نیازارد is a negative present/past‑general verb meaning 'does not harm / does not molest' (classical usage often renders a general statement). او را is the object marker 'him' carried by the verb. کسی here, in the scope of the negative, functions as 'no one' (negative polarity). زین سپس is a temporal adverbial 'after this / thereafter' (زین = 'from this', سپس = 'then/afterwards'), together providing the time frame.
segments.s479-g72: ازو در جهان is a locative/source phrase 'from him / in the world' (classic word order places the source before the verb). یافتم is 1st‑person singular past 'I found'. داد و بس is a noun phrase 'justice (داد) and sufficiency/enough (بس)' — i.e. 'I found in him, in the world, justice and sufficiency.' Note داد here is a noun 'justice' rather than a past‑tense verb.
segments.s479-g17: نویسم is a 1st-person present/subjunctive form of نوشتن (here used narratively as “I write/compose”). The verb comes first and takes the following noun phrase as its object: یکی نامه دلپسند = “a certain/one pleasing letter” (یکی as an indefinite determiner before the noun; دلپسند is an adjectival complement following نامه).
segments.s479-g18: که introduces a subordinate clause describing the letter: here 'that/which'. کیدست is a predicate with the copula -ست meaning 'is a plot/stratagem'. تا expresses purpose 'so that/in order that' and governs the subjunctive باشد 'may be' (present-subjunctive form), with او 'he' as the subject and شاه هند the predicative phrase 'king of India'.
segments.s479-g19: This nominal phrase functions as the subject of the clause: خردمند نه مرد رومی — an emphatic negative coordination 'no wise man, no Roman man' (نه linking the two nouns). برفت is a past-tense verb meaning 'went away/left' (classical poetic past).
segments.s479-g20: ز پیش = 'from before / from the presence of' (poetic z + پیش). سکندر is the proper name 'Alexander' in the oblique. سوی is the preposition 'toward'. کید is the noun 'plot/stratagem' and here governs the following element as a descriptive complement; تفت appears after کید and in this context names the locus/phrase tied to the plot (the given lexicon senses don't match well, so its precise lexical sense is uncertain here).
segments.s479-g21: چو here = 'when'. سالار هند is a noun phrase 'the commander (or lord) of India'. آن سران را is the definite object 'those leaders' with the object marker را. بدید is the past tense 'saw' (from دیدن). The clause thus means 'when the Indian commander saw those leaders.'
segments.s479-g22: فراوان is an adverb 'much/many/a great deal'. بپرسید is a past-form (poetic) of پرسیدن meaning 'he asked' (i.e., he put questions). و = 'and'. پاسخ is 'answer/reply', the object of شنید 'he heard' (past). The sequence means 'he asked many questions and heard the reply.'
segments.s479-g23: چنانچون = 'just as / as (it) was' (introducing a conforming clause). ببایست is an impersonal/analytic past meaning 'it was necessary' or 'as was needed'. بنواختشان is a transitive past form with the 3rd-person plural object suffix -شان 'them' and here means 'struck/caused to be struck/played' (contextual choice: 'struck' or 'caused to sound'); the verb is linked to ببایست to mean 'as was proper, he had them struck/played.'
segments.s479-g24: یکی جای شایسته is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain/well‑fitting place' (یکی = a certain, جای = place, شایسته = fitting/appropriate). بنشاختشان is a past verb with object suffix -شان 'them' meaning 'he set/placed/installed them' (here transitive 'set them in place'); word order is determiner + noun + adjective followed by the verb with object clitic.
segments.s479-g41: This is an elliptical, contrastive clause: خردمند (the wise man) is set against پیر (the old man) by the short negative/contrastive نه placed before پیر. مانده is the past intransitive 'remained/stayed' referring back to خردمند (implicit subject), and به جای is a fixed locative phrase 'in/at place' meaning 'in his place' or 'standing still'. The overall sense is 'the wise stayed (but) not the old.'
segments.s479-g42: زبانها پر از is the idiom 'tongues were full of' (زبانها subject, پر از = 'full of'). آفرین is used as an exclamation/noun 'praise, hooray' rather than a verbal root, and خدای here is vocative/associated with the exclamation ('O God' / 'praise to God'). Word order is topical (زبانها first) and the phrase describes their verbal reaction.
segments.s479-g43: نه ... یکی forms a negative quantity: 'not one.' جای گذر 'a place of passage' (a way to go/leave). دید is the past 'saw.' ازیشان here (از + ایشان) means 'from/of them' and combines with یکی to indicate 'not one among them.' Word order is emphatic/poetic: the negator precedes the quantified noun.
segments.s479-g44: نه زو is a negated prepositional phrase: ز (از) + ـو (short form of او) = 'from him'; here it means 'not from him / they did not (move) away from him.' چشم برداشتند is the compound past 'they removed/withdrew their eyes' (برداشتند, 3rd plural past). اندکی is an adverb 'a little' modifying برداشتند; overall: 'they did not take their eyes off him even a little.'
segments.s479-g45: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' فرزانگان is the plural subject 'the sages' and دیرتر مانده­ند is the past/pluperfect idea 'remained/lingered longer' (ماندند, 3rd plural past). The clause gives the temporal setting: 'when the sages stayed on longer.'
segments.s479-g46: This is a coordinated result/action clause. کس آمد is a simple past 'someone came.' بر + شاهشان indicates direction/target 'to their king' (شاهشان = شاه + possessive -شان). خواندند is 3rd plural past; here it is best read as 'they called/summoned' (the sages summoned the king), so the agents of خواندند are the previously mentioned فرزانگان; the two clauses are reported in sequence.
segments.s479-g47: چنین گفت is a common formula 'thus he said.' با + رومیان + شهریار marks the addressee/party: با here functions like 'to' or 'before' in classical usage, so 'he said thus to the Roman ruler.' The verb گفت is past third-person singular (implicit subject).
segments.s479-g48: که introduces the content of what was said ('that' / introducing a question). چندین is a quantifier 'many/several.' چرا here functions as an interrogative 'how/for how long' in poetic usage (i.e., 'how many [times/periods]'); بودتان is the past copula with the 2nd‑person plural clitic 'you' ('you were' / 'your state was'), and روزگار is 'time/fate.' Together this yields the sense 'how long / how many periods has your fate been so.'
segments.s479-g1: گزین کرد is a past tense verbal phrase (گزین + کرد) meaning “chose/selected” with the auxiliary-like کرد following the verb-stem; word order is verb first (poetic) then the source phrase زان رومیان (’from those Romans’) and the noun phrase مرد چند (’a few men’). چند after مرد is an indefinite quantifier “several/a few.”
segments.s479-g2: This is a string of coordinated adjectival/epithet phrases describing those chosen men. خردمند (wise) begins the list; بادانش is the fused form of با دانش, meaning ‘with knowledge/learned’; بی‌گزند is an adjective meaning ‘without harm’ or ‘blameless.’ The coordinating و simply links the qualities.
segments.s479-g3: یکی here is an indefinite subject ‘someone/a certain one’; نامه is the direct object ‘a letter’ placed before the verb (common in poetic prose); بنوشت is an archaic/simple past form of نوشتن (he/she wrote) with prefixed ب- used in older Persian — it is the main verb. پس شهریار follows as an oblique phrase identifying the addressee (’to/for the king’) or indicating sequence ‘then, to the king’ depending on context.
segments.s479-g4: پر از is a fixed descriptive phrase meaning ‘full of’ (پر = full, از = of). The nouns joined by و list the qualities that fill the letter: پوزش ‘apology/pardon’, رنگ ‘colour/appearance’, بوی ‘scent’, نگار here as ornament/image; the coordinating و links them.
segments.s479-g5: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause (‘who/that’). نه is the negation used in a comparative construction here (‘not less…’); نامور ‘renowned’ is the predicate; ز (poetic form of از) introduces the standard/comparee ‘from/among his steadfast ones’ (استواران خویش). The suffixed ش (خویش) marks possession ‘his own.’
segments.s479-g6: ازین is the fused poetic form of از این (‘from/among these’), where از is the preposition and ین = این. پرهنر (‘full of skill’ or ‘highly skilled’) modifies نامداران (‘renowned/illustrious ones’); خویش (خوی+ش) again marks ‘his own.’ Overall this phrase supplies the second term of comparison: ‘than among these very skilled famous men of his.’
segments.s479-g7: A run of coordinated qualities describing the men: خردمند ‘wise’; بادانش (با+دانش) ‘with knowledge/learned’; شرم here is positive ‘modesty’ (not merely ‘shame’); رای means ‘opinion/counsel’ (i.e., of good counsel). The repeated و links these epithets in a catalogue typical of epic style.
segments.s479-g8: A second list of complementary attributes: جهانجوی ‘world‑seeking’ (ambitious for worldly affairs), پردانش ‘very learned/well-informed’ (پر‑دانش), and رهنمای ‘guide/adviser’. The final noun ties back to the earlier listing as further appositive description of the chosen men.
segments.s479-g9: فرستادم is a first‑person singular past tense verb ('I sent' / 'I dispatched'). اینک is an adverb meaning 'now' placed after the verb for emphasis. به + نزدیک + تو is a prepositional phrase 'to/for someone near you' (به = to/for; نزدیک = near; تو = your/you), here indicating the recipient ('to your close one').
segments.s479-g10: نه پیچند is a negative jussive/subjunctive: نه (not) + پیچند (3rd person plural present/subjunctive of پیچیدن) giving the sense 'may they not twist' or 'do not let them twist'. با رای باریک تو is an instrumental phrase 'by/with your subtle/delicate counsel' (با = with; رای = counsel/plan; باریک = delicate/subtle; تو = your), which specifies the means by which twisting might occur.
segments.s479-g11: This is an imperative clause addressed to 'تو' (you). این چیزها را is the direct object ('these things' + the object marker را). بدیشان is an indirect object/benefactive phrase 'to them' (بِ + ایشان contracted), and نمای is the present imperative/subjunctive of نمودن/نمود (here 'show' / 'perform'); together: 'You show these things to them.'
segments.s479-g12: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/indeed'. بباشد is a jussive/subjunctive 3rd‑person singular of بودن used optatively ('let it be' / 'may it remain'); the implicit subject is the things just mentioned. هم‌انجا ('right there') + به جای ('in place') form a locative phrase specifying where they should remain: 'let them remain right there in their place.'
segments.s479-g13: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when'. من is the subject 'I'. نامه is the object 'a letter'. یابم is first‑person present/subjunctive of یافتن/یافتن used in the temporal clause ('I obtain/receive'); after چو the subjunctive is common for a temporal subordinate clause. ز پیران خویش is a prepositional phrase 'from my elders' (ز = from; پیران = elders; خویش (خوی + ش) = 'one's own', here 'my').
segments.s479-g14: جهاندیده and رازداران are appositive descriptive nouns modifying 'پیران' (the elders): جهاندیده = 'worldly‑wise' / 'wide‑seeing', و = 'and', رازداران = 'secret‑keepers' or 'confidants'. خویش (again split as خوی + ش) repeats 'my/their own' referring back to the speaker's circle (i.e. 'my worldly‑wise ones and my confidants').
segments.s479-g15: که introduces a relative content clause here ('that'). بگذشت is a past tense verb 'passed' / 'came before' (lit. 'went past'). بر چشم ما is a locative/experiential phrase 'before our eyes' (بر + چشم + ما), and چار چیز is the subject/phrase 'four things' (چار = four; چیز = things) which is what passed.
segments.s479-g16: که begins a subordinate clause giving the quality of those things: 'which no one in the world had.' کس را here is the logical experiencer/indirect object marked by را (کس in negative sense 'no one' in this context). به گیتی = 'in the world' (به = in/at; گیتی = world). نبودست is an archaic past negative 'did not exist / had not'. نیز at the end is emphatic 'also/indeed' reinforcing that no one else possessed them.

sentences.s479-s25: He was that very man, a human with a (handsome) face; in beauty he possessed a share from every star.
sentences.s479-s26: The Roman said to him: 'O king, in the iwan no one sees such a beauty.'
sentences.s479-s27: Now, let each one of the moon‑formed send a single letter to the king.
sentences.s479-s28: Then the philosophers sat together and took paper, tar, and pen.
sentences.s479-s29: Each mobad wrote down what he had seen — that the sheet of paper had been ruined and disappeared.
sentences.s479-s30: From near them a horseman rode off to Alexander, to Milad Taft.
sentences.s479-s31: When the king of the world read their letters, he remained astonished at their speech.
sentences.s479-s32: In the letter they had described each of his limbs/features, but only briefly.
sentences.s479-s13: The next day, when the sky had turned yellow, the sun rose and lifted its battle-sword.
sentences.s479-s14: They adorned that daughter of the king; the moon need not (even) adorn itself.
sentences.s479-s15: He set a golden throne inside the chamber and placed folds of ornament all around.
sentences.s479-s16: She sat upon the throne, her face more radiant than Venus in the heavens.
sentences.s479-s17: They went — alert, not old men; smooth-tongued, eloquent, and quick to learn.
sentences.s479-s18: The king sent them toward the bride, at the summons/word of Alexander Filqous.
sentences.s479-s19: The elders saw the king’s daughter’s face; it shone, and about her were an attendant, a throne, and a seat/place.
sentences.s479-s20: They stood there dumbfounded and staring; at the sight of her the old man's foot became weak.
sentences.s479-s33: The sovereign wrote them in reply: “Bakh‑bakh — I saw a joyful paradise.”
sentences.s479-s34: Now return with four things; do not seek any increase beyond these.
sentences.s479-s35: When you give him my charter and covenant, you shall place upon him (with) faghestān a benah and clothe/equip him.
sentences.s479-s36: Thereafter no one harmed him; in this world I found in him justice and sufficiency.
sentences.s479-s9: I compose a pleasing letter — a stratagem — so that he may become king of India.
sentences.s479-s10: No wise or Roman man went forth from Alexander's presence toward the place of the plot.
sentences.s479-s11: When the commander of India saw those chiefs, he put many questions and heard the reply.
sentences.s479-s12: As was fitting, he caused them to be struck, and he set them in a suitable place.
sentences.s479-s21: The wise remained in place, not the old; their tongues were full of exclamations of “O God!”
sentences.s479-s22: Not one of them saw a place to pass; they did not take their eyes off him even a little.
sentences.s479-s23: When the sages lingered longer, someone came and they summoned their king.
sentences.s479-s24: Thus he said to the Roman ruler: 'How long has your fate endured so?'
sentences.s479-s1: He chose from those Romans a few men — wise, learned, and blameless.
sentences.s479-s2: Someone wrote a letter to the king — a letter full of apologies and of color, scent, and ornament.
sentences.s479-s3: who was no less famous among his own steadfast followers than among these highly skilled, renowned men of his.
sentences.s479-s4: Wise and learned and modest and of counsel — ambitious for the world, learned, and a guide.
sentences.s479-s5: I have now sent it to someone near you; may they not twist it with your subtle counsel.
sentences.s479-s6: You show these matters to them; indeed let them remain right there in place.
sentences.s479-s7: When I receive a letter from my elders — my worldly‑wise and my confidants.
sentences.s479-s8: Four things passed before our eyes that no one in the world had.
